# tokens: # Structure — non-scalar values inside Nodes, resolved at depth
# Companion to nodes.tsv. Where nodes.tsv covers the structural
# mechanics of List/Map (construction, auto-eval, quoting) with mostly
# scalar contents, this file focuses on what happens when the *values*
# inside a structure are richer: type literals, record-shape maps,
# enums (disjuncts), Function values, FunctionSignature type values, and atoms —
# and especially on `def` references to such values resolving
# arbitrarily deep inside nested Lists and Maps.
# Notes on a couple of rough edges:
#   - Function and FunctionSignature *values* stringify verbosely (they carry
#     whole signature structs), so rows that embed them check the
#     containing Node's `typeof` / `is` rather than rendering the
#     value itself.
#   - A `def`-bound *function* referenced bare inside a structure
#     auto-invokes (the FnDef-literal auto-call rule) rather than
#     embedding the function value — so `def f fn[…] {x:f}` is an
#     error, not `{x:<fn>}`. To embed a function value, write the
#     `( fn … )` literal inline.
#   - Object / record *instances* (via `make Type …` /
#     `make RecordType …`) need the production constructor words
#     (`object`, `record`) which aren't in the kernel — those are
#     covered in lang/spec/object.tsv and lang/spec/record.tsv. A
#     'record' in the structural sense `{ field:Type … }` (a map all
#     of whose values are type literals) IS a kernel construct and is
#     exercised here.
# === Test fixtures (eng/go/spec_test.go::registerSpecWords) ===
#   addq / subq / mulq  — [Number Number] forward-prec — args[1] op args[0]
#   lengthq             — [List] forward-prec — list length
#   firstq              — [List] forward-prec — first element or None
#   Pre-bound defs: pi=3, tau=6, greeting='hello'.
# === end fixtures ===
# 1. Type literals as structure values
# A type literal (Integer, String, …) is a perfectly good Node value.
# A list of type literals; a map whose values are type literals (the
# latter is a record-shape — see §2).
[ Integer String Boolean ]
note: list of three type literals

# tokens: lengthq [ Integer String Boolean ]
firstq [ Integer String ]
note: first element is the Integer type literal

# tokens: { a:Integer }
note: single type-literal value

# tokens: { a:Integer b:String c:Boolean }
note: (source already in sorted-key order)

# tokens: # def / type references to a type literal resolve when the structure
# auto-evaluates.
def t Integer { x:t }
note: `t` (bound to Integer) resolves as a map value

# tokens: def t Integer [ t t String ]
note: resolves element-wise in a list

# tokens: def IdType Integer { id:IdType }
note: a type-named binding referenced as a value

# tokens: def IdType Integer [ IdType String ]
# 2. Record-shape maps as structure values
# `{ field:Type … }` — a map all of whose values are type bodies — is
# a record-shape (a Node-family TYPE). It can sit inside another Node
# as a value; nesting goes to any depth.
{ schema:{ id:Integer name:String } }
note: a record-shape as a map value

# tokens: { schema:{ id:Integer name:String } } typeof
note: the whole thing is itself a record-shape → metatype Type

# tokens: { rows:[ { id:Integer } { id:Integer } ] }
note: a list of record-shapes as a value

# tokens: { outer:{ middle:{ inner:Integer } } }
note: three-deep record-shape nesting

# tokens: { schema:[ Integer String ] }
note: a typed-tuple-ish list as a value

# tokens: # def / type references inside record-shapes resolve at depth.
def t Integer { schema:{ id:t name:String } }
def IdType Integer { schema:{ id:IdType } }
def t Integer { rows:[ { id:t } { id:t } ] }
# 3. Enums (disjuncts) as structure values
# `enum [ … ]` builds a Disjunct type. Embed it directly with a paren
# (`{ k:( enum … ) }`) or — more usefully — name it with `def` and
# reference the name.
{ e:( enum [ red green blue ] ) }
note: disjunct value as a map value

# tokens: { choices:[ ( enum [ a b ] ) ( enum [ c d ] ) ] }
note: list of disjuncts

# tokens: firstq [ ( enum [ a b ] ) ( enum [ c d ] ) ]
# type-named enum referenced at depth.
def Color enum [ red green blue ] { c:Color }
def Color enum [ red green blue ] { palette:[ Color Color ] }
def Color enum [ red green blue ] { deep:{ inner:{ kind:Color } } }
def Color enum [ red green blue ] firstq [ Color ]
# 4. Functions as structure values
# A function VALUE (the `( fn … )` literal, not a def-bound name)
# can be a Node element. It stringifies verbosely, so these rows
# inspect the containing Node rather than the function itself.
{ h:( fn [ [ x:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ x addq 1 ] ] ) } typeof
note: a map whose value is a function is still a Map

# tokens: { h:( fn [ [ x:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ x ] ] ) } is Node
[ ( fn [ [ x:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ x ] ] ) ] is List
lengthq [ ( fn [ [ x:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ x ] ] ) ( fn [ [ y:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ y ] ] ) ]
note: a list of two function values

# tokens: # A def-bound function referenced bare inside a structure auto-invokes
# (the FnDef-literal auto-call rule) instead of embedding the value —
# so this is an error, not `{fn:<function>}`.
def double fn [ [ a:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ a addq a ] ] { fn:double }
note: `double` auto-calls with no args inside the map literal

# tokens: # 5. FunctionSignature type values as structure values
# `fnsig [ … ]` builds an FunctionSignature (function-shape) type value. Like a
# function value it stringifies verbosely, so these rows check the
# containing Node.
{ s:( fnsig [ [ Integer ] [ Integer ] ] ) } typeof
{ s:( fnsig [ [ Integer ] [ Integer ] ] ) } is Map
def Mapper fnsig [ [ Integer ] [ Integer ] ] { schema:{ sig:Mapper } } typeof
note: an FunctionSignature nested two levels deep; the outer value is still a Map

# tokens: # 6. Atoms (quoted words) as structure values
# `( quote name )` captures `name` as an Atom — the clean way to put a
# symbolic value inside a structure.
{ tag:( quote red ) }
{ tags:[ ( quote red ) ( quote green ) ( quote blue ) ] }
[ ( quote x ) ( quote y ) ]
# def-bound atom referenced at depth.
def a ( quote red ) { tag:a }
def a ( quote red ) { deep:{ inner:[ { x:a } ] } }
def a ( quote red ) lengthq [ a a a ]
def a ( quote red ) firstq [ a ( quote green ) ]
# 7. Def references at depth — the core 'go deeper' cases
# A `def` name used as a value resolves wherever it appears
# in a nested List/Map, however deep.
def t Integer { a:{ b:{ c:t } } }
note: three maps deep

# tokens: def t Integer { a:[ { b:[ { c:t } ] } ] }
note: map → list → map → list → map

# tokens: def t Integer [ [ [ t ] ] ]
note: three lists deep

# tokens: def t Integer { rows:[ { cols:[ t t ] } { cols:[ t ] } ] }
def Color enum [ a b c ] { ui:{ theme:{ accent:Color base:Color } } }
note: type-named disjunct, three deep, twice

# tokens: def a ( quote red ) { x:{ y:{ z:[ { w:a } ] } } }
note: atom, four deep

# tokens: # Mixed: a config-shaped structure combining a type, a disjunct, an
# atom, and a paren-evaluated number, all at depth, plus a def
# reference resolving inside it.
def n 2 def Color enum [ red green blue ] { schema:{ id:Integer kind:Color } defaults:{ count:( n addq 1 ) tag:( quote red ) } }
note: `n` resolves; `Color` resolves to its disjunct; Integer stays a type literal; quote stays an atom

# tokens: # Auto-eval still mixes freely with these: a paren-evaluated scalar
# value alongside a type-literal value in the same nested map.
def n 5 { row:{ value:( n mulq 2 ) type:Integer } }
